data.title: Cresud's Standalone Operations Are Unprofitable, Not An Opportunity
data.description:
Cresud (NASDAQ:CRESY) released Q2 2025 earnings. The conglomerate consolidates the earnings of IRSA (IRS) and Brasilagro (LND), which I have already analyzed for Q2 2025 in other articles (Irsa, Brasilagro).

The company's specific business, which is agriculture in Argentinian lands, and two other consolidated subsidiaries in agricultural services are not doing very well. The Argentinian agricultural sector is coming from a bad 2024 and what seems like a bad 2025 as well, with low prices, an appreciated currency, and high costs.

Based on a sum-of-the-parts methodology, I do not think CRESY is attractive, especially considering that at least two of its large subsidiaries can be accessed separately and that the assets of the standalone company are unprofitable. I maintain a Hold on the company.

Cleaning The Q2 2025 Results Reporting

As commented in previous articles, none of the companies of the Cresud Group is very good at reporting in a way that is clear for investors to understand what is happening in the business.

In the case of Cresud, this is magnified by the consolidation of businesses on which it has control but not a super majority (Irsa 55%, Brasilagro 35%, FyO 50%, Agrofy 17%). In addition, we have Argentinian hyperinflation accounting mixed with a particular dollar-based revaluation method of properties.

We start with a segment-based separation, which already helps us ignore part of the income statement. The Urban Properties and Investments below is the consolidation of Irsa (analyzed separately in another article), so I believe we can ignore it.

From the Agribusiness segment, we need to remove the results of Brasilagro's H1 2025 campaign, which, to make matters worse, are presented in BRL by Brasilagro but in ARS by Cresud.

In H1 2025, Brasilagro generated revenues of R$430 million, a gross profit of R$200 million, and an operating income of R$131 million, including land sales. This translates to about AR$86 billion (R$1 ~ AR$200) in revenues, AR$40 billion in gross profits, and AR$26 billion in operating income.

If we remove those figures from the Agribusiness above, we get AR$152 billion in revenues, -AR$17.2 billion in gross profits, and -AR$17.6 billion in operating income. These figures are not very different (at the operating income level) from Cresud's Q2 2025 standalone financial statements, which were released only in Spanish. The difference (mainly in revenues) comes from the consolidation of the other two agricultural operations that are not consolidated in the standalone statements (Agrofy and FyO).

Bad Year For Argentinian Agriculture

The reason for such bad results is that Argentinian agriculture is not in a great period. Soybean prices have been low for 2/3 years already, and Argentinian costs have grown over 100% in USD for the past year because of the peso appreciation policy of the current administration.

Agricultural producers receive less from their exports (low prices, low exchange rate), and their inputs and costs grow exponentially (high local inflation). This dynamic was well described in Bioceres' Q2 2025 earnings call, showing terrible results in Argentina as well (I covered it here).

The only positive for the Argentinian agriculture sector was the temporal decrease of export duties announced this year, which will be valid until June. This adds about 10% to the final price of exports (the ad valorem tax was reduced from 33% to 26% on soybeans), or about three or four months of Argentinian appreciation.

Sum Of Parts Valuation Is Not Attractive

We can remove the traded items from Cresud's balance sheet and find whether or not the assets owned at the conglomerate level are attractive.

35% of Brasilagro is worth $133 million, and 55% of Irsa is currently worth $600 million. These two add to $733 million. The company also has recorded about $365 million in net dollar-denominated debts on its standalone financial statements. This means the net financial assets of the conglomerate are about $370 million.

The company also has ownership in at least two non-traded subsidiaries, but those operations are included in the calculation I made above.

Cresud trades for $723 million, which, after removing $370 million in net financial assets, results in an EV of $353 million.

Compared to this, the company is generating operating losses in H1 2025, which are similar to the operating losses in FY 2024, FY 2023, and FY 2022 (standalone financial statements).

That means the assets of the conglomerate cost $353 million on the market but have generated operating losses for the past 3 or 4 years.

I do not think that is an opportunity, and for that reason, maintain a Hold on CRESUD as a conglomerate. The traded assets can be accessed separately. I believe they are not an opportunity, but some readers might disagree.

data.title: Getting PUMPed Up On ProPetro (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
ProPetro Holding Corp. (NYSE:PUMP) reported essentially in-line Q4 2024 results, provided a solid outlook for pressure pumping for 2025, and further elucidated plans for targeting and growing their new power segment (PROPWR). We continue to see the name, whose sole focus is on the Permian, which we view as the most resilient basin in the US, overly discounted vs. their pressure pumping and power peers. This is even as they maintain a strong balance sheet and channel free cash flow to growth and share repurchases.

Our expectation is that tightness in the pressure pumping market will develop in Q2 and Q3, coinciding with their first power segment revenues drawing more attention to the story. Lastly, we view the Street as overly conservative for 2026.

Key Points This Quarter

Pressure Pumping (Frac)

We would characterize the overall U.S. frac market in 2025 as stable, with pricing that's below 2024 but with an upward tilt from current low levels of activity. We view the Permian and especially the higher end of the market where ProPetro operates as more resilient. This is a function of the blue-chip nature of the upstream players they serve, the discipline displayed by the company and other players in terms of not adding new capacity, and the ongoing attrition in lower-end horsepower that is chewed up at an increasing rate by the demands of bigger, more intense fracs.

PUMP kept an average of approximately 14 frac spreads active in 2024, and in Q1 2025 plans to run 14 to 15. They, like their peers, see rising activity in Q2 and Q3 '25, potentially tightening the market later this year. This is ahead of what we expect to be a tighter still market in 2026 as new capacity continues to replace and retire older fleets instead of being incremental to existing capacity. At present, PUMP's fleet of approximately 16 spreads is 75% natural gas capable. It's either dual fuel or electric (their four FORCE efrac fleets). A 5th FORCE fleet is scheduled to arrive mid-year, adding to revenues and EBITDA in the second half.

Notably, on the fourth quarter call, management spoke to longer-term contracting of their fleets. We've seen this with eFleets, but less often with Tier IV or less capable fleets.

Power (PROPWR)

The company has placed orders for 140 MW for the new power segment, and they plan more orders this year. They expect to have 150 to 200 MW in service around yearend 2025, with the first units arriving mid-2025. Our understanding is that the preferred markets for their power will be oil and gas production (production (lift)), oil and gas midstream, and their own frac fleets. For reference, their four Force fleets require 165 MW. In the future, industrial and data center demand, which are on the rise in the Permian, can be addressed by PROPWR as well.

Management is in talks now with blue-chip Permian players for production and midstream needs. They expect this to be long-term in contracts (3+ years). We don't see much risk of the assets being purchased and then idled, given demand in the Permian is set to far exceed supply. At least one bigger player is planning to add small modular nukes to meet their long-term needs.

On the call, management put the impact on EBITDA at $0.3 to $0.4 mm per year per MW, similar to comments made by Solaris Oilfield Services (SEI). SEI's nascent power segment has driven sharp gains in their share price since late last year on thoughts of power demand for data centers and AI. Our view is that the Street is not factoring in this kind of growth in the 2026 EBITDA estimate from just the low end of the expected MW range.

Valuation: This table provides more insights as to what we mean when we write that they are cheap vs. their traditional peers.

Nutshell

We don't see the forward multiples as giving enough value to their oil service lines or as giving much if any value to the new power segment. And we see the Street is likely modestly underestimating 2026 EBITDA (by 10 to 15%). Regardless, even at the current estimate, the name is overly low vs. the peer group frac peer group despite a fortress balance sheet and ongoing share buyback. We'd then note that new to the power segment, Solaris is trading closer to 5x. Not that long ago, Solaris was a sand logistic play.

As ProPetro's power segment transitions from dream to reality, we expect the name to gain traction with a broader audience. We see PUMP's metric moving from the low-3xs to low-4xs, taking the name to $12 over the next 6 to 12 months.

data.title: CGW: Upgrading This Essential Industry From Sell To Hold
data.description:
When I joined this platform in late 2022, I started by analyzing ETFs I have some history with. There are about 100 I followed very closely for many years, when I used to manage "other people's money." Now that I'm the client, I try to search under as many proverbial rocks as possible, and report my findings as I go. And with ETFs rapidly accelerating in popularity (crossing $11 trillion in assets seemingly right after hitting $10 trillion), I am going back to some old favorites as well as some newbies.

With Invesco S&P Global Water Index ETF (NYSEARCA:CGW), I can say truthfully that I have been the least successful analyst here in covering this nearly $1 billion play on a clearly essential industry. I have also been the most successful analyst. Because I'm the only one who has written about CGW in the past 2 1/2 years. I placed a sell rating on it both times, in November 2022 and again in February 2023. Since that first rating, CGW has gained 22.5% including its annual dividend of about 2% a year. That trailed the S&P 500 by more than 30%.

CGW primarily invests in stocks classified in 1 of 2 sectors: Industrials and Utilities. It has underperformed the former by a lot since my pair of sell ratings, but has been at least competitive with Utilities, though behind there as well. So, why revisit it here? A few reasons:

Why Follow This "Boring" Industry?

1. This is an essential industry. While energy has had its own debate among climate change folks on both sides, the process of keeping water potable and available is an even more dire situation. We don't drink oil.

2. This is one of the industries that gets less investor attention than it deserves. Not because it might be a "buy" or a "sell" now, but because in the same way that investors always keep their eye on the energy sector, even when it is in the dumps investment-wise, I view water in the same way.

3. This is a global ETF, and the US Dollar concerns me. That means that any ETF with a global presence is automatically more intriguing to me than it has been in years.

And, while I landed on a "hold" for CGW, it is an upgrade from sell, and speaks to my view that while this is still not a thrilling sector, I think it should at least be "in the running" at all times. As noted above, I've owned it in the past, going back several years. And I'd rather have it stowed away on my ETF watchlist than out of sight completely.

A report from October 2024 from the World Meteorological Organization emphasized several key messages that speak to the sustainable aspect of this business:

    • 2023 was driest year for global rivers in 33 years

    • Glaciers suffer largest mass loss in 50 years

    • Climate change makes hydrological cycle becomes more erratic

    • Early Warnings for All must tackle water-related hazards

    • WMO calls for better monitoring and data sharing

Water is not an infinite resource. There's quite an irony there, as I'll discuss below. For now, note that CGW includes a range of industries, as described in the ETFs' prospectus:

Materials, chemicals, commodity chemicals, industrial inorganic chemicals, water treatment chemicals, industrials, capital goods, construction and engineering, construction and engineering, heavy construction, water, sewer and pipeline construction, specialty contract work, water well drilling, consumer discretionary, consumer durables and apparel, household durables, household appliances, water appliances, water treatment appliances, utilities, water utilities sectors.

The fund is concentrated, though not as much as many ETFs I follow. Still, I like my ETFs to be at least 50% across the top 10 when possible, and this one gets about that bar, despite having more than 70 holdings. I am also just fine with a turnover rate that looks like an active manager is running this (which is not the case). Stocks in modern markets behave in a way that encourages me to not be as patient as 10 or even 5 years ago. I don't think I am rewarded as consistently now. Maybe that will change, but investing is about evolving with markets, not clinging to traditions. Just ask bond bulls, and utilities investors that still think of those stocks as stodgy. Not with the price action we've seen this decade!

As noted above, industrials and utilities are the main drivers here, at about 90% of assets. And the top 10 holdings show that just 5 stocks account for about 1/3 of CGW's total portfolio. Again, that's a plus for me, in part because I can look at those leaders within the larger portfolio as potential single-stock candidates for my portfolio. I am very "equal opportunity" between ETFs and stocks. I just want to make money consistently without major declines along the way. If you are thinking "no wonder Rob likes boring industries like water treatment" you are absolutely right!

The global nature of CGW's portfolio is shown below. Note I said "global" and not "international." Global implies a strong US presence/core, with some non-US stock diversification. That's what we see below. It is mostly Europe, with some emerging market exposure. But 80% of the ETF is a triumvirate, if you will.

I like to review these top 25 tables and include them in some of my research reports because they remind us that index ETFs like this one are simply predetermined stock baskets. That's the allure of this investment type to me. The ability to look through and analyze it like a portfolio, albeit with a limited scope.

For CGW, I see above that valuations are higher than I could put a buy rating on. And below the leading contributors include a couple of non-US stocks, so that global mix is contributing to results.

Below, at 25-26x earnings, close to market multiples, this fund is not cheap but not outrageous either. And the sales multiple of 1.6 is fairly attractive. So is that projected earnings growth. This all feeds into my calculus at concluding a hold rating, up from a sell, is in order.

The yield is "meh" to me at 2.2%, but that growth rate might be a tip off for future consideration. That type of dividend appreciation is hard to find.

CGW: Upgrade To Hold

Valuation says hold to me, the ETF has been pretty stable price-wise, and the global angle is a helper. Not enough for me to scream "buy" here, but plenty of "weight of evidence" in the formulaic way I approach these vehicles to upgrade CGW to hold. I will certainly continue to track this ETF. Even if no one else in this space does.

data.title: Talkspace: Missed Earnings Hurt Shares, 'Ifs, Buts, And Maybes' Hurt Business Model
data.description:
Investment Overview

The Telehealth, or Virtual Care, industries have been a major disappointment for retail investors. Hopes were high when Teladoc Health, Inc. (TDOC) stock ran up to $290 per share during the pandemic. The market believed it would accelerate the use of such platforms. However, it appears that neither patients, physicians nor insurers wanted to engage, and Teladoc stock is worth <$15 per share today, while other telehealth companies — for example, Babylon Health (now insolvent), Ontrak, Inc. (OTRK) (shares down >95% on 5-year basis), and American Well Corporation (AMWL) (shares down 98% over the same period) — have suffered even worse fates.

The only sector that has proven “remotely” successful is behavioral/mental healthcare. Consumers have taken to online counselling, perhaps because it is cheaper, more convenient, and more private than visiting a psychologist/psychiatrist/counselor in real life, and there are no physical examinations required.

According to a presentation from Talkspace, Inc. (NASDAQ:TALK) — the subject of this post — given at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare conference in January, 46m, or 23% of adults in the US, have a mental health condition. 25% of 66m Medicare patients have a mental health “challenge,” plus 2m active military, and 10m teens. Talkspace estimates these markets are worth $46bn, $16.5bn, $2bn, and $1bn respectively.

In reality, Talkspace remains a <500 employee company whose stock has taken a hit today. This was after reporting a Q4 revenue miss and only $2.7m of adjusted EBITDA. However, the recent upswing in the share price of Teladoc — up >75% across the past six months — whose key asset is its mental healthcare business BetterHelp, may suggest the market is finally beginning to warm to this sector.

In this post, I'll take a closer look at Talkspace's Q4 and full-year 2024 results, and study its strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats as we look ahead into 2025 and beyond. We will speculate about whether buying today's dip may pay off eventually.

Talkspace 2024 Earnings In Review — Switch To Payor Model “Paying Off”?

Talkspace describes itself as follows in its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings press release:

Talkspace pioneered the ability to text with a licensed therapist from anywhere and now offers a comprehensive suite of mental health services, including therapy for individuals, teens, and couples, as well as psychiatric treatment and medication management (18+).

With Talkspace’s core therapy offerings, members are matched with one of thousands of licensed therapists within days and can engage in live video, audio, or chat sessions, and/or unlimited asynchronous text messaging sessions.

All care offered at Talkspace is delivered through an easy-to-use, fully encrypted web and mobile platform that meets HIPAA, federal, and state regulatory requirements.

More than 179 million Americans have access to Talkspace through their health insurance plans, employee assistance programs, our partnerships with leading healthcare companies, or as a free benefit through their employer, school, or government agency.

For the full-year 2024, the company reported $188m of revenues, up from $150m in 2023 - a gain of >25%, but towards the lower end of previously shared guidance for $185-$195m. Quarterly sequential growth in 2024 was less impressive, however, with revenues being $45.4m, $46.1m, $47.4m and $48.7m in quarters one to four respectively.

Gross profit and gross profit margin were trending in the opposite direction, from $21.7 and 47.8% margin in Q1, to $21m and 45.5% in Q2, $21.6 and 45.6% in Q3, and $21.5 and 44.2% in Q4.

This in itself is not necessarily a bad sign, however, as Talkspace management puts it down to “a shift in revenue mix towards payor.” In 2023, the company earned $81m of its $150m of total revenues from the payor, $34m from direct-to-employer (“DTE”), and $26m from direct-to-consumer (“DTC”). In 2024, $124m of revenues were payor, $38m DTE, and $25m DTC. Payor sessions increased from 851k in 2023, to 1.23m in 2024.

The company was able to report net income of $1.2m, its first-ever profitable quarter, up from a $(1.3m) loss in Q4 2023, and adjusted EBITDA of $2.7m, up from a $(0.3m) loss in the prior year.

During the presentation given at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference, Talkspace's Chief Financial Officer (“CFO”) discussed the company's decision to prioritize payor over DTC as follows:

This gets back to the strategic decision that Talkspace’s board made two and a half plus years ago, that to sustain the direct-to-consumer model requires just such substantial capital deployed towards marketing because of the higher churn.

Don't get me wrong, great gross margins, but higher churn rates, shorter retention. Think of it as cheaper calories. You got to keep filling that funnel on the marketing side. We made the investment decision quite frankly to take the pain. We took it in the public markets. Look at our P&L, look at our stock price, I think that in 2021, '22 time frame reflected that.

This is a key point of differentiation from Teladoc's BetterHelp business model, which is heavily reliant on Internet advertising to bring paying consumers to the platform. Talkspace's payor revenues increased 45% year-on-year in 2024, with covered lives increasing 40% to 160m people, and total sessions up 38% to 316k. Speaking on today's earnings call with analysts, the CEO told analysts:

We continue to make progress on our goal to be in-network for all Medicare beneficiaries. As of this month, we are now CMS-approved in approximately 40 states and live in 30 states, including Texas, Florida, California, and New York, therefore providing access to the majority of the senior population in the U.S.

Our team is working diligently to complete the necessary final state approvals and launch our services in the remaining 20 states so that we can begin to test our go-to-market strategies more broadly.

In addition to traditional Medicare, we are pleased to report expansion into Medicare Advantage with our October 1 launch announcement into the nation's largest Medicare Advantage plan, furthering our reach to another 7 million plus lives.

Looking Ahead: 2025 & Beyond

After thirteen years as a company and three years as a listed company, Talkspace went public in 2021 via a deal with Special Purpose Acquisition Company “(SPAC”) Hudson Executive Investment Corp. It initially valued the company at $1.4bn (share price of $10). Talkspace now appears to have settled on a long-term strategy of making its service free for individuals, with payors covering the cost.

Presumably, as usage by users with access to the service increases, payors will have more and larger bills to settle. As the CEO put it during the JPM presentation:

Keep in mind the payor model unlike our direct-to-enterprise and unlike our legacy D2C revenue model is completely on a fee-for-service basis. From a clinical perspective, from a member perspective, from a payor perspective, our business model in this segment is completely aligned with trying to drive utilization. So as you think about 2025, a lot of the sort of technology and product investments we're making are around retention, what we call session velocity.

Intriguingly, although Talkspace boasts a “curated network of ~6k experienced providers”, management showcased its AI credentials, at JPM, via an AI-generated personal podcast designed for a teenager experiencing bullying after sharing a picture of themselves on social media.

The “podcast” taps into all the data collected via Talkspace's platform by human therapists, and produces a note-perfect advice session for the teenager in question. Presumably, this is also a far cheaper alternative to paying a human therapist.

Talkspace management compares the payor coverage it has to a “hunting license,” all it has to do now is persistently remind teens, Medicare plan members, and members of the military, that they have completely free access to Talkspace's platform.

All of this progress has not necessarily translated to surging growth. 2025 guidance is for $220m-$235m of revenues, and adjusted EBITDA “in the range of $14m-$20m”. On a 3-year basis, the company is forecasting revenue growth of >20% - >25%, and >12% - >15% EBITDA margin growth.

Concluding Thoughts: So Many Ifs, Buts & Maybes, Plus Tricky Set Of Earnings Make For A “Hold” Rating

A few weeks ago, after Talkspace stock surged from $3 per share to $4 per share, in part due to sharing its FY24 guidance for $185m-$195m, I suggested to members of my Seeking Alpha investing group, Haggerston BioHealth, that the stock looked set for further gains in 2025.

Today's plunge in the stock's value as revenues landed at the lower end of guidance is somewhat unfortunate, but does it make me reassess my “bull” thesis, or sense a “buy the dip” opportunity?

On one hand, Talkspace's forward price-to-sales ratio of ~2.3x is low enough to make a compelling bull case. On the other hand, earnings per share for 2024 of $0.01 translates to a price-to-earnings ratio of ~300x, and even if we look at forward price-to-EBITDA based on 2025 guidance, the ratio is >30x.

Talkspace's revenues may grow by >20% in 2025 — if guidance is met — but I would not describe this business as “fast-growing” either from a top-line or bottom-line perspective. The key question, however, is can it become fast-growing?

I can certainly see what Talkspace is trying to do. Secure as many covered lives as possible via deals with payors, then chase up these warm leads and remind them they have access to a completely free service. Then it can provide a strong enough service to ensure retention rates remain high, using AI to streamline costs and using intelligence gathered by the platform.

It makes sense, but plenty of businesses that “make sense” go on to fail. As recently as March 2023, Talkspace stock was languishing at <$0.75, so there is a long way for the stock to fall if the company cannot manage to persuade people to use its services.

It may all come down to quality of service, and there are some encouraging signs here — apparently, management revealed during the JPM presentation:

Based on some new technology efforts, we've seen an 18.5 percent decrease in no-show rate, a 30 percent increase in patients completing three sessions within one month, and an increase of 15 percent in sessions per member.

Another key factor is whether Medicare and the military will use the service. In my view, teens who are used to text messaging and communicating online are best suited to Talkspace's services. However, their access will be funded by schools, which I suspect have less budget for “fee-for-service” activities than employers, the government, or the military. Therefore, it is essential that Talkspace can capture the adult market, and I have some doubts about that score.

In summary, I think there are too many “ifs, buts, and maybes” to support a full “Buy” rating for Talkspace stock at this time. The company has done well to survive the devastating bear run on telehealth stocks across the last few years, and like Teladoc, its share price has been buoyant, up 85% on a 6-month basis.

Today's sell-off reminds us that the entire sector is still skating on thin ice, however. In 2025, we may discover which strategy — Talkspace's payor strategy, or Teladoc / BetterHelp's advertising / DTC strategy — pays off. Could it be both? It's possible, but my doubts around the willingness of what Talkspace sees as its biggest market — Medicare and military — to start texting with AI bots to improve their mental health — means I'm reverting to a “hold” call, despite some clear signs of undervaluation.

data.title: SoundHound AI: I'm Getting Out Before Earnings And You Should Too
data.description:
By Alex Kim

I got into SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) in late October for $6.1. It was a small holding in my long-term portfolio. It was pure speculation on what, I thought, could be a huge turnaround.

They had a solid value proposition with their AI and I thought profitability was around the corner. When the stock went up I took profits on the table and I sold 30% of the holding for $19. My original plan was to hold the rest for the next two years, catching the ride on the voice AI wave as SoundHound established its dominance. But times changed.

I’m getting out before earnings, and here’s why.

SoundHound’s fundamentals don't justify its value even after the 28% drop recently. They're losing money at a pace not viable and still haven't been able to find a means of earning stable profits. Also, recurring dilution of shareholder value is a massive red flag.

And when top artificial intelligence company NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) divested its complete stake the stock fell more than 28%. That's not noise from the institution. I am reading this as a warning. If Nvidia doesn't find value in the long term, why should I?

I’ll walk you through why I’ve decided to sell my remaining shares and why you might want to consider doing the same. The red flags are stacking up and the risk/reward ratio shifts in such a way that remaining invested becomes a risky bet.

The company's $25.1 million Q3 2024 top-line and 89% YoY growth rate could be passable on paper, but there was a whole range of problematic matters under the radar.

Most critically, this growth is inorganic from SYNQ3 and Amelia deals and not organic growth of the core business. SYNQ3 contributed $2.8 million and Amelia contributed $15.6 million in two months. The organic growth environment looks much worse if you exclude these two deals. Subtracting Synq3's contribution ($2.8M) and Amelia's contribution ($15.6M) then comparing the remaining "organic" revenue of ~$6.7M to the prior year's Q3 revenue of $13.3M. If you exclude these two deals, organic growth of the core business declined by approx. 50% YoY.

This decline in organic growth is even more disconcerting in the context of their positioning in what should be a growth space for voice AI. The decline has been partly created because of fluctuations in the firm's relationship with its largest automotive customer, which previously generated significant one-time licensing revenues. While they tried to diversify their revenue stream from their largest customer from 72% down to 12% YoY, this has resulted in a significant penalty on profitability.

The margin drop is steep and demonstrates both integration challenges and a movement towards lower-margin lines of business. GAAP gross margins dropped from 73% in Q3 2023 down to 49% in Q3 2024.

Even after adjusting for amortization on acquired businesses, non-GAAP gross margins declined significantly from 74% down to 60%. The compression is mainly from acquired companies' significantly lower-margin profiles, with SYNQ3's call centers being a major example.

Although management expects margins to be regained through cost rationalization, the complexity of consolidation and the period for achieving such gains remains uncertain. Their operating losses also significantly deteriorated, more than doubling to $33.8 million in the Q32 FY24. The reason this is bad news is that what this means is even if the company's making more money, they're still not generating a profit. To this, their adjusted EBITDA loss also rose, from $7.3 million in the Q3 of 2023 to $15.9 million in the Q3 FY24.

If you compare these working troubles with the background of SoundHound's valuation then it's heavily overvalued even after a 28% drop on a single day on its current valuation. I, personally, would not invest even a single penny more than $5 in it.

It is trading on EV/Sales of 62.6x (TTM) 1,571% higher than the industry average of 3.5x. The 50x P/S and 14.5x P/B also suggest extreme optimism, and I believe this is becoming increasingly decoupled from reality.

This type of extreme growth multiples is generally limited to the highest growth, highest-margin software companies. The top-end multiples are even more daunting when comparing their profitability with industry peers. The firm's EBIT margin of -149% and EBITDA margin of -132% compared with industry medians of 5.5% and 10.6%, respectively.

They are bleeding money hand over fist, with a negative net income margin of -163% and a negative return on equity of -72%. Clearly, their business model isn't working. They are not creating shareholder value, they are destroying value. Management says they will turnaround and be adjusted EBITDA profitable in 2025. But with the mounting deficits and ongoing cash needs, this target isn't feasible in my opinion.

Cash Burn, Dilution, and Competition

The company spent way more money in the first nine months of 2024 than in 2023 $75.8 million compared to $54.4 million. That's a 39% increase. Their adjusted EBITDA loss for Q3 FY24 was $15.9 million, more than two times $7.3 million for Q3 FY23. That means the journey towards profitability becomes more challenging and not easy.

To fund such escalating losses, they've had to repeatedly access equity markets, causing substantial shareholder dilution.

Diluted shares outstanding skyrocketed from 242 million in Q3 FY23 to 360 million in Q3 FY24, 50% of diluted shares in a single year. Recently, they've raised $150 million under a $150 million ATM program and announced a fresh $120 million ATM facility, and hence more dilution can be expected.

While the $135.6 million of cash and cash equivalents on the books of the firm as of Q3 2024 must be balanced against the burn rate of the firm and its debt obligation, they owe $39.7 million of debt with an onerous 15% rate of interest, and there is a chance they might pursue another equity raises for refinancing this expensive debt.

With their burn rate and debt service requirements, I estimate the cash runway of the firm to be around 18 months without other financing, with nothing changing in the operating deficits.

Despite management's claims of technical competence, the restaurant space where they've been focusing their growth efforts exhibits a very fragmented industry with a number of participants achieving similar levels of performance.

For instance, Hi Auto's solution has been lived in 350 of the Checkers and Rally's, and they are realizing a 95% order accuracy rate, quite similar to what SoundHound has said. Wendy's teamed up with Google, and they are bragging about a 99% rate of accuracy with their ordering with artificial intelligence. Presto Automation also has 95% accuracy with Del Taco.

The absence of differentiation with regard to technology becomes problematic when you compare the firm's competition with deep-pocket giants of the industry. Amazon, Google, and Apple not only have enormously more resources, but also benefit from massive voice data and established customer bases.

In the automotive space where SoundHound has been focused, the company's top-line growth has been growing modestly, but I don't consider this growth impressive because it doesn't turn into net income which means they won't be able to scale it profitably suggesting a potential share gains for its larger peers.

While management has diversified its revenue concentration figures with the largest customer being only 12% of revenue this year against 72% for the earlier year, this diversification has not been inexpensive. A substantial proportion of today's revenue stream has lower-margin services, not to mention approximately one-third of revenue from low-margin and non-recurring professional services. This then raises questions about the quality and sustainability of the revenue stream.

I think organic growth can remain negative until 2025 and be masked by the contribution of M&A. The voice AI opportunity remains appealing, SoundHound's declining economics and use of equity for financing, however, leave huge doubts about its future and its ability to achieve substantial market share without recurring shareholder dilution.

Risks to My Thesis

My bearish opinion of SoundHound AI can be countered with various possible factors for the upside. The greatest of these factors is the likelihood of the company realizing its SYNQ3 and Amelia acquisitions' synergy more rapidly than I expected.

Management has cited Amelia has created significant enterprise customer relationships, including seven of the global top ten banks. We haven't seen anything from them yet. But if they can sell their voice AI solutions into these accounts and achieve anticipated cost savings from data center migration and technical stack consolidation, the current $155 to 175 million 2025 revenue guidance could be low.

The voice AI space itself can be expected to be more rapidly adopted, and of note, in the restaurant environment where they are achieving 90% order accuracy rates. The 100 million interaction mark recently passed signals scale growth, and growth with major brands including Chipotle and White Castle signals industry adoption growth. The notion of their AI solutions being able to drive upselling rates of up to 60% (as compared with 5-15% from human personnel) represents a very appealing value proposition for returns on investment and could drive more rapid adoption.

SoundHound has a significant intellectual property portfolio with more than 155 granted patents and proprietary solutions such as Speech-to-Meaning and Dynamic Interaction that may be of value to a strategic suitor. With shares trading well down from their highs, a larger technology firm may find value in acquiring their stack of technology and corporate connections instead of developing similar competencies in-house.

Bottom Line

Some investors might see the recent price decline following Nvidia's exit as a buying opportunity, but I believe it more likely represents the beginning of a larger valuation reset as the market recognizes these fundamental challenges. So, I exited my position on Friday ahead of earnings and I recommend other investors consider doing the same.

data.title: The Trump Economy 3 Weeks In
data.description:
This is Part I of a two-part article (due to the length). Part II will be submitted shortly, and the articles will be linked for reader convenience.

Introduction

This article is an update to an article I wrote and published on Seeking Alpha on November 11, 2024, shortly after the election but well before President Trump took office entitled, “The Trump Economy.”

We only have three weeks of actual evidence upon which to base our updated analysis, but it is far more than we had in November. A lot has transpired, in terms of executive orders, cabinet appointments, and DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) findings. Still, only a small fraction of the executive orders has been fully implemented and efforts to reduce inefficiencies and excessive spending are just getting underway. But we are seeing trends that will begin to shape the future of federal government spending and can make a few assumptions as to what the impacts will be once steps to implement have been completed. Yet, I think we all get the feeling that there is a lot more to come.

I have tried to focus only on those actions that will affect the economy and will leave social issues to sort themselves out as many will have minimal, if any, economic impact on the macro level. Also, I have attempted to take the high road (in Part I) by looking at the possible outcomes from an optimistic perspective. For example, I have assumed that DOGE will be able to reduce the 2026 deficit by at least $1T. Having spent much of my career as a federal government employee, I was always aware of significant waste. Preliminary findings suggest potential areas of financial mismanagement, so finding $1T in savings from a $6T budget seems very possible to me.

Additionally, I take the position that actions such as tariffs have multiple outcomes, some good, and some bad. The good may or may not be enough to offset the bad, but it should help reduce the pain making the path to the eventual goals tolerable in the short term.

I will take a less optimistic approach in Part II but will not jump to “the sky is falling” conclusions because the administration can make corrections along the way when necessary to keep the worst-case scenario from becoming reality.

Inflation

We need to remember that the recent CPI and PPI readings were taken around the middle of January before the new administration took office. We also need to be reminded that the impact of actions taken during the first few weeks in office for a new President will not be measurable for several months because the bureaucracy moves slowly, especially when completely changing directions. A few months will be required to fully implement executive orders in most cases.

So, most of the reports on inflation and the economy will be driven by two factors, laws and regulations already in place on January 20th and the perceptions of how changes that are being made currently will impact the economy in the future. There are a lot of moving parts, so perceptions will change over time.

With that said, let us look at some charts that paint a picture of where we were as of January (the starting point). Headline CPI MoM (Month over Month) surprised to the upside driven by several factors. First, the Biden Administration was doing all it could to push as much appropriated money out the door as possible before Trump took office, fearing that the spigot could get shut off (which has happened to a certain degree in many areas).

The following charts are from The Daily Shot which I receive via email five days a week. Each email contains more than 100 charts that illustrate the U.S. and global economies. The Daily Shot is a subscription-based service, but well worth the relatively modest cost compared to the plethora of information provided.

Next, we see that the Core CPI MoM reading was also higher than consensus expectations.

The 3-month change (annualized) came in at 4.46%, which is well above previous readings.

Much of the rise can be attributed to a few big increases in such areas as auto insurance, used car prices, and prescription drugs as shown in the charts below:

Increased auto insurance rates are being driven primarily by losses incurred due to hurricane flooding and wildfires. Wildfires are also responsible for much of the rise in used car prices, especially in California.

Pharmaceutical companies, as a group, often raise prices each year on most existing drugs. It occurred in a big way this January.

Housing makes up about 35% of our CPI calculation in the US. Owners' equivalent rent and housing rental inflation remains too high for inflation to get down to the Fed’s 2% target. I find it interesting that this element makes up a much larger percentage of CPI in the US compared to how CPI is calculated in other developed countries.

Moving on to PPI (Producer Price Index), the story of higher than expected continues, but there are at least a few bright spots if we dig a little deeper.

US PPI was higher than expected but down from the prior month. Both Hospital In-patient Care PPI and Physician Care PPI were negative in January.

And US Trade Services PPI was much lower.

The reaction to these two reports was interesting. When the CPI report came out, the US bond market yields shot up, but when the market digested the PPI report, those same yields fell.

We are basically back where we were prior to the CPI report. I watch the bond market, especially the 10-year Treasury yield, to inform me about what to expect from the economy. I find that bond analysts are far more accurate in predicting the next 6 months to a year for the economy compared to equity analysts. But even the bond market is volatile these days.

The market can climb a wall or worry as long as the problems facing the economy are understood. But markets do not like uncertainty; meaning that the future of the economy is in doubt because there are too many factors that could change. That is precisely where we are today.

Tariffs

As I mentioned in my previous article,

"The question is whether he will move quickly or if the level of tariffs he spoke about during the campaign were meant as bargaining chips to use in negotiations, as he did with Mexico and Canada in 2016."

I still stand by that assessment because, thus far, President Trump has used the threat of blanket tariffs on Canada and Mexico to extract concessions. He may still enact the tariffs in March if not enough measurable progress has occurred at the border. The goals are two-fold: secure the borders against illegal immigrants and stop the flow of drugs across the borders into the US.

The 10% tariff on Chinese goods was a shot across the bow to bring them to the negotiating table before imposing harsher tariffs. However, trade with China has been impacted more by the elimination of the de minimis shipments of under $800 to enter the US duty-free. That will hit billions of dollars worth of small packages shipped to the US every month. It will also make US-made goods more competitive.

The State Department also needs to renegotiate the agreement between the USPS (US Postal Service) and China because our USPS loses money on every package shipped from China for delivery in the US. It costs Chinese shippers less to ship a package from China to anywhere in the US than the cost for US domestic shippers to have USPS deliver a package within the US. (I am aware of this because I was a senior accountant at USPS Headquarters during part of my career.) That agreement is decades old and was negotiated when China was considered a developing economy. The US State Department created incentives and advantages to encourage greater trade with China and to help their economy develop. I don’t think China needs our help anymore.

Tariffs on steel and aluminum will hurt Canada the most, since it is the largest exporter of steel to the US, by far. Again, I believe that this is a negotiating tactic that could eventually lead to quotas, ensuring that US steel and aluminum producers will retain a reasonable share of the domestic market. If a war breaks out that we are drawn into, we will need healthy steel and aluminum industries. This is as much a national defense move as it is a trade balance issue.

We will learn more about the President’s trade and tariff policies in March, when it will be decided whether actions taken by Mexico and Canada have been adequate.

In terms of economic impact, tariffs can be a dual-edged sword. On one side, we would expect at least some higher costs to be passed on to consumers through higher prices. It is also likely that sellers (foreign exporters) will absorb a portion of the tariff costs through lowering prices in order to remain price competitive and retain market share.

On the other side of the equation, we need to consider the impact of currencies. Usually, tariffs on imported goods will tend to strengthen the currency of the importing country relative to the exporting country. If that happens, some increased costs due to tariffs will be reduced by a stronger US dollar. Either way, the full cost of the tariffs is unlikely to be borne fully by US consumers.

Other factors affect the relative value of the US dollar compared to other major currencies, and we will dig into those later in Part II of the article.

DOGE Impact

This could be the most consequential economic impact if (and it is a big IF) Elon Musk and DOGE are as successful as they believe they can be.

According to Fox News,

DOGE posted on Jan. 28 that the group is "saving the Federal Government approx. $1 billion/day, mostly from stopping the hiring of people into unnecessary positions, deletion of DEI and stopping improper payments to foreign organizations, all consistent with the President's Executive Orders."

"A good start, though this number needs to increase to > $3 billion/day," DOGE added. DOGE hasn't provided a detailed breakdown of its cuts, so it's unclear how it is calculating the savings it is claiming.

Musk claimed on Friday that he was "cautiously optimistic that we will reach the $4B/day FY2026 reduction this weekend."

He previously suggested that cutting an average of $4 billion a day in spending would be needed to reduce the federal deficit by $1 trillion in fiscal 2026, but he didn't post a follow-up about whether the goal was achieved.”

The Administration and DOGE have a three-step process for downsizing government employee numbers. First, all employees were encouraged to voluntarily resign and continue to receive full pay through the end of September 2025 (the end of the government fiscal year). Approximately 75,000 accepted the offer and resigned by the deadline.

Second, all agencies, and departments have been instructed to terminate the employment of new employees who are still under probation (which can last up to two years, depending on agency policies). Several agencies have already begun this process, with more than 10,000 employees already being terminated so far.

Third, most agencies (not including Homeland Security, DEA, ICE, other agency personnel being used at the border or in deportation efforts, and the Postal Service) have been instructed to prepare for a RIF (Reduction in Force) process to eliminate unnecessary jobs and non-essential personnel. Such actions generally require reducing the total employment number by at least 5% for each agency involved.

The goal is to reduce the total number of federal government employees by at least 10%, or 200,000. Finally, the administration has initiated a freeze on hiring and in the future agencies must eliminate 4 employees for every new hire. That is intended to keep agencies from increasing staff again to ensure cuts are more permanent (for at least the next four years).

One of the more interesting findings unearthed by DOGE was the number of extremely old people continuing to receive social security benefits. In the entire world, there are 42 people confirmed to be 112 years old or older. At first glance, it appears that our Social Security Administration is sending monthly checks to over 6,000 people over that age. Apparently, the coding is antiquated and is creating errors in the reporting, but it remains possible that people died, relatives failed to report deaths and SSA failed to catch it. Imagine how many DOGE may be able to identify between the ages of 75 and 112. The potential financial malpractice within SSA could amount to tens of billions of dollars per year. Savings of that magnitude could extend the viability of Social Security for several years. This matter requires more analysis to be certain what is the truth. The American public deserves to know.

According to the Social Security Administration, "All improper payments, including payments to the deceased and the very old, are estimated at about $3 billion per year." It says that fixing the code would be too expensive to bother with. I am not sure, but it would seem to me that saving $3B per year should warrant some coding upgrades.

But, according to the Inspector General of the Social Security Administration, over a period of 8 years nearly $72 Billion was reported as improperly paid and the Administration did nothing to fix the problem. That amounts to an average of $9B per year. Now we are beginning to talk about real money.

There could be waste or financial malpractice within Medicare and Medicaid, too. Finding it will be less straightforward and is less likely to be on the insured side; rather, it will be in the medical claims by insurers and practitioners. Again, the potential for huge savings just by eliminating waste or potential fraud could be in the $ Billions.

Then there is the Department of Defense. Merely by the sheer size of the spending, there is ample opportunity for potential fraud and waste. FBI, CIA, and IRS; all will be audited, and these agencies are undergoing increased scrutiny to ensure financial transparency. All the research funded by the many different agencies will be audited to determine whether the research is beneficial to American taxpayers.

USAID (United States Agency for International Development) has a budget of over $40B annually. The list of grants to foreign entities is long, and so much of it has absolutely nothing to do with US taxpayers. Helping people in an African nation prepare their taxes is benevolent and compassionate, but do we really have the extra money available for such things? Should we be collecting taxes from our citizens so we can spend that money to educate people in other countries about LBGTQ+ and DEI? I suppose if our government had paid all its debt, and we were running at a surplus instead of a deficit, those nice to-do items would make sense. But spending $40B on goodwill that really benefits neither the recipients nor our own citizens is, in my humble opinion, unnecessary. Maybe if we were feeding people who would otherwise be starving or providing medical aid. The review of such programs seems prudent with our debt skyrocketing. Common sense, please.

Fortunately, one of the programs from USAID that purchases food from our farmers and provides needed assistance to the poor is being saved and transferred to USDA (UA Department of Agriculture). So, the good things will survive, but the waste is being eliminated.

On Valentine's Day, DOGE proclaimed that it had recovered $1.9B in funds that had been “misplaced” by HUD (Department of Housing and Urban Development).

DOGE is just getting started. There are over 400 federal government agencies, many of which were intended to be temporary to operate during the crisis but are still receiving annual appropriations and spending with no real purpose other than to pay employees and collect data that is no longer needed. In other words, there is a lot of low-hanging fruit before DOGE starts trying to trim spending by making agencies more efficient.

What if DOGE is successful in eliminating over $1T in spending for the 2026 fiscal year? For starters, the US dollar would strengthen against other major currencies if the US could reduce its annual deficit by that much in one year. That would make most imported goods less costly in dollar terms. Do not forget that we import a ton of stuff.

Next, it would create greater confidence in our government’s ability to repay its debt which, in turn, would lower interest rates on US Treasury debt by reducing the risk premium investors would require to buy Treasuries. That would then slow the increase in the interest payments the government pays to roll over old debt and issue new debt. It would also create greater confidence in the private sector by reducing the cost of borrowing, thereby incentivizing more capital spending in the US. All these things would support a healthy, growing US economy.

DOGE is designed to be disbanded at the end of 18 months. The eventual goal is to eliminate $2T in annual spending by the US Government. It will require a lot of work and collaboration with Congress.

Speaking of Congress, the House Budget Committee debated the issue of tax cuts and came away with an agreement framework. Tax cuts will be dependent upon cuts in spending.

“It calls for $1.5 trillion in spending cuts, with an additional caveat: The House package has to include $2 trillion in cuts to the “mandatory” part of federal spending, which covers programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and food benefits known as SNAP or $4.5 trillion amount in tax breaks must be reduced by a commensurate amount.”

Those numbers are measured over a ten-year period. If DOGE is successful in eliminating $1T or more per year from spending, tax cuts could be increased, or the deficit will be reduced. I vote for the latter.

Part I Summary

Inflation remains persistent at the end of the Biden Administration. Major increases in prescription drugs are unlikely to continue beyond January but used car prices and auto insurance could remain high due to natural disasters. Unless the housing component of CPI falls, the core CPI will remain elevated, and the Fed could have difficulty meeting its target of 2% in 2025. However, if DOGE is successful, the prospects for lower interest rates and lower inflation in 2026 appear to be much better.

Tariffs, thus far, have been used primarily as a negotiating tool to establish a position of strength from which to obtain concessions from trading partners. It is unclear at this point whether higher tariffs will be enacted going forward, or if further negotiations will take place. If more tariffs are levied against our main trading partners, inflation could rise in the short term (6-12 months), but the longer-term effects will be difficult to determine due to other factors such as reduced deficit spending by the US government and how that will impact the strength of the US dollar relative to the currencies of our trading partners.

A lot is riding on the success of DOGE to eliminate waste and potential fraud from government spending. It will be interesting how the courts rule on the many lawsuits that have been brought by opposition to the Trump Administration’s methods to make government more efficient and transparent. It will also be interesting to see whether DOGE and Congress can work together to bring spending under control. If everything gets bogged down in the courts, spending cuts could be delayed, and the outcome could be worse than projected.

The markets are likely to be volatile in 2025 with companies still dependent on supply chains based in China experiencing rising costs. If the US dollar rises, multinational corporations domiciled in the US will have earnings eroded by currency translation. US companies that primarily serve domestic customers could benefit by becoming more price competitive relative to foreign-sourced competition.
